item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, this reminds me of a Doctor who episode!

item.title: Updated Image Source
item.content:encoded:
Since the Wikipedia article for 91st Street Station changed the image, here's the source from Wikimedia Commons:

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Street_grading_defunct_91st_St_station_WTM3_The_Fixers_0003.jpg

item.title: Re: Third option
item.content:encoded:
but aren't agents and researchers who fell from grace with the foundation demoted to D-class personel?

No?

I mean, some people might believe this, but personally I feel like it's a bad idea. Not only does it waste the skills of the personnel member demoted, it's also a potential security liability since D-Class aren't supposed to have any significant security clearance or knowledge of the Foundation's inner workings.

item.title: Re: Third option
item.content:encoded: Sorry bout the necro, but aren't agents and researchers who fell from grace with the foundation demoted to D-class personel? There you go then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
.. That was probably the least subtle redaction I've ever seen, and the least interesting one. So guys become -C's. Why was that said in such a way? It seems unnecessary.

Otherwise, pretty good. Faceless things from an older era wanting to revert our world to their time period is kinda cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great job! No violence but it still manages to be interesting and slightly creepy, and that's always a plus for me. One question though: what happens if someone tries to force his way into a station? What happens if the D-Class personnel do something wrong in the safe world? Will that trigger a more hostile behavior? Also, the researchers should probably ask themselves not only who made this world and why, but also WHEN it was made. It seems like whoever made it was trying to leave our world, and the motivations behind that are an interesting mystery that could be tied to the time during which this whole world was built. That being said, it would be cool to add a document addressing this. Was this world really made in the period it tries to resemble, or was the whole 1959 aesthetic a choice of its creator? Nothing too explicit but a peek into the research would be awesome.

item.title: Re: Holy shit, this got featured!
Holy shit, this got featured!
item.content:encoded: Beside myself with pride. I don't use the site much anymore (drift in and out, usually) but I was stopping by today and was thrilled to see an SCP of mine on the front page.

item.title: Third option
item.content:encoded: As to me, I believe D-Class ARE recruited from Death Row Inmates, but the "terminated a month after recruitment" part of the job is a lie the higher-ups tell new researchers when they start working with D-Class so the new guys don't feel bad when D-classes die on the job. My headcanon for the D-Class in this interview is that not only does she avert The first 3 bullet points (since despite being a Death Row inmate, she's female; she managed to finish preschool, elementary school, junior high school, high school, & all 3 levels of college all with perfect grades including such on extra credit assignments; & has no mental illnesses whatsoever), but she happens to be completely innocent of any of the crimes that would get someone on Death Row, & was only on it in the first place due to being framed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: NEVERMORE.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Belated reply.

The best way to think of it is a looped pocket dimension with self-aware inhabitants. The world of 2135-B is a home for the creatures that inhabit it, whatever they may be. They demonstrate an awareness of the passage of time, indicating that it's not a memory-erasing loop.

It's important to note, however, that observation of 2135-B is impossible without directly entering it (I didn't mention it in the article, but it's impossible to indirectly observe 2135-B via satellite uplink). It's entirely possible that time in the pocket dimension continues on past 1 Feb…but not when there are outsiders in it. The 24-hour time limit is a defense mechanism - and because it's impossible to make any observations after a day has past, it's also impossible to know what happens in 2135-B when it's left unobserved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had my attention at "SAFE WORLD"… and then, once I thought I knew what was going on, you threw "PRACTICE" at me. Nicely done; upvoted. (I like this better than 2727 anyway.)

The really basic thing I'm still wondering about is time in 2135-B. It always looks like February 1 1959 when you get off the train… but if you stay too long, it will tick over to February 2, as evidenced by the station being closed, and it won't change back. So how do those fit together? Are you visiting a different B every time you get off the train? Do the trains take you back along B's timeline, so that each train arrives on Feb 1 1959 regardless of when it left, and you could meet yourself if you went in twice? Does B's internal calendar reset between visits in some way that we haven't witnessed? (If so, do objects and inhabitants revert to their previous state on their own, or do the inhabitants just put everything back where it was?) If I were a researcher, I would immediately want to test this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would have been better if the article just said that instead of hiding it behind a [REDACTED]. It doesn't make it more mysterious or scary, just more annoying to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems to strongly imply that if you get left in -B for too long, you become a -C. But it's hard to tell since you no longer have facial features.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I'm not calling plagiarism or anything, just pointing out that there is a lot of very similar material on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. Was aware of 2727 but hadn't really looked over it fully. All similarities are coincidental.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, so…

It's very similar to SCP-869 in terms of initial conception, containment procedures, and New York-iness (Although if it's not on Coney Island, it's probably New Jersey-ness, and I sincerely apologize for the implication.)

The other world is fairly similar to SCP-1165 (among others, I think) in the boundaried back-of-the-world idea.

I don't mind the redaction listed above. It's not perfect redaction, but it does leave me room in my head to play around with what happens with people who stayed there, which is a big part of the fun of both 869 and being in a weird semi-Earthly other dimension.

Ultimately, I do like this. It's well put together and the interview log is good, even if the twist it adds is… not bland or overdone, but a little basic.

I just don't know if it stands out enough from things on the site. No vote for just now. I'll give it another read when I've had more sleep, see if I have a more solid impression.

EDIT: Also, SCP-2727, which, while narratively/tonally different, has a huge amount of stylistic overlap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh, I liked it because it has different imagery and atmosphere from other, similar skips? Not sure what there is to explain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shorter than I expected, but I liked it anyways. My one gripe though, is this:

[REDACTED] although said reports remain unconfirmed, and the similarities may be superficial or coincidental.

This seems rather useless since it isn't telling me anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's open to interpretation, but I think SCP-2135-C existed prior to the station's closure, and said closure disrupted their existence somehow.

Don't want to say too much because I want to see what others come up with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've always favored a similar approach myself, where they have multiple sources of D-class- there are several skips (and mostly mundane super-technology developed by groups like Promethius Labs) that allow cloning, and then for others where it might matter that you're dealing with a non-standard human, they use the death row inmates.

Either way, they aren't free, and there's of course the ethical side of clones still being people, so you still get the whole "D-class do not equal meat robots" thing, but it also solves the previously mentioned "only so many death row inmates" thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you maybe explain? I can't really make out anything except that they want to escape from the real world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go whichever route you which; I personally headcanon the multiverse approach for D-Class and finances because it's the only way for the Foundation to maintain the resources and budget necessary to contain the sheer number of SCPs it does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if they're not dangerous criminals? What if the Foundation is taking low-level inmates when they're eligible for parole and making them the D-Class offer then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is getting a bit off-topic, but I don't go the standard route RE: D-Class depictions. My headcanon is that they're taken from alternate universes and dosed with amnesiacs, thus giving the Foundation an unlimited supply of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looking it over, I've decided to upvote anyways. You did a great job of holding my attention until the interview, so there you go.

+ 1 until further notice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure, and that's why I asked the author about this. I was actually surprised by the answer myself.

+ show block
– hide block

That said, my real point is that the D-Class conducting the interview seems very unlikely to me. She's a 29 year old woman, seems to be trusted by the research staff enough to to this and seems to be a native English speaker.

  • Only 2% of death row inmates are female.
  • Only 48.2% have even a High School education.
  • 10% are seriously mentally ill.
  • All of them are convicted of serious murders and in most states must either be mass murderers or have "special circumstances" that make the crime especially heinous (like prolonged torture).

Or she's one of those refugee or political prisoner ones, I guess. In that case, I'm not sure what's motivating her to do this (and I also personally think this "other" D-Class should be considered a lot less disposable from an ethical stand point).

I guess I don't think it makes sense to send an unreliable and disposable person to do this job, but rather somebody with some training and loyalty. I also think I don't like using the slave labor testing force, except when you really must.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's different. One's period appropriate, the other is not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking, eh, we've got enough skips like this, but it's the nature of the second world and that interview, not the method used for getting there, that's the real draw. Happily upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There was something I was attempting to imply with the interview log, and some people have guessed what it meant (won't confirm that one way or the other).

And the instances don't stop you from leaving before 24 hours; they stop you from entering the non-91st subway stations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I posted it in the Forums (a second ago before I saw this) I'll move it here.

+ show block
– hide block

Lil' something inspired by my interest in the NYC subway system:

My first good draft, later abandoned for no particular reason was inspired by this as well. I must read.

I liked it until the interview, which was confusing. I'm sorry I don't have more to say but the interview doesn't seem to add anything and I couldn't tell what it meant. Is there something I'm missing? Reflecting back on it, the world depicted didn't give me much a "wow" either. The clinical wording is good, but capital letter signs and instances that attack/block you when you try to leave is a bit straight forward and, frankly, random.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed. Muchas gracias!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They had people using unfamiliar slang sometimes too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They had people with accents or English as a second language in New York prior to 1959. (Ellis Island and all that.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No items manufactured prior to February 1959 (including clothing, recording equipment, entertainment media, etc) may be taken into SCP-2135-B.

Do you mean manufactured after this date?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, so the only D-Class that can do this job are political prisoners or refugees with American accented English?

A lot of those, do you suppose?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What happens when the invasion starts? I suppose they can only invade one subway train at a time, though…

Although, I guess this is fairly close to Site-28 and the City Slickers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, they just stop interacting and stare at you until you leave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no real telling what someone who ate their family or murdered a whole church or whatever is going to do. I certainly wouldn't expect the typical convicted mass murderer or special circumstances murderer to be able to follow the instructions about slang (they weren't able to follow the much simpler instructions about not eating their families). I suppose these could be exclusively D-Class from refugee camps, but then I'd think you might have some problems with English proficiency. Do they react to foreign words or accents the same way they do to non-period ones?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: IMO, closed circuit cameras are sufficient for surveillance. If anyone enters they can just send security in to escort 'em out. Given that the station is abandoned, hardly anybody comes anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personnel in city maintainence uniforms. Surveillance gear. Tactical teams based out of sight of the platform.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because the anomaly is still unpredictable to a degree, and is unsafe after 24 hours.

Also, researchers can enter, they just need approval for entry from the higher ups.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed.

Also the numbers are sort of a stylistic thing I have in most of my skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The 91st Street Station is still visible to people passing it via train, so you can't really guard it without causing a secrecy breach

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"fifteen (15) seconds" "fifteen (15) minutes"

You don't need the (15), and really makes the article harder to read for me.

SCP-2135 is currently closed to the public and inaccessible by conventional means; as such, this is considered to be a reasonable request that does require excessive coercion on the part of the Foundation.

Does require, or doesn't require?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. It's not super creepy, but subtly so, and it's got mystery. The interview log feels like it's lacking something, though. I'm not quite sure what. Overall, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really get the specific examples of non-period slang. Why those particular words?

Also why a D-Class explorer? This seems fairly safe, why not send some actual researchers and agents? With D-Class and no enforcement mechanisms, I'd be worried about escape attempts or committing crimes in the alternate world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote for sure, but why are foundation personnel discouraged from guarding it? It would seem that once the foundation discovered that the occupants of SAFE WORLD were planning an invasion of our world that they might want to keep some resistance in place and civilians out of harm's way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
New SCP, inspired by my interest in the New York City Subway system (and this short story).

Photo is from Wikimedia Commons and is available here.
